title: Mutabal
subtitle: Smoky charred aubergine whipped with tahini, lemon, and garlic.
excerpt: The Levantine cousin of baba ghanoush, mutabal blends charred aubergine flesh with tahini, lemon, garlic, and a touch of yoghurt into a smoky pale dip. The pith of the flavour is in burning the aubergine skin black; everything after that is twenty minutes.
sourceNotes:
Levantine home cookery, particularly Lebanese, Syrian, and Palestinian kitchens where mutabal and baba ghanoush are everyday mezze. Documented in twentieth-century Arabic cookbooks and in Claudia Roden's Book of Middle Eastern Food.

Mutabal and baba ghanoush are siblings, not the same dish. Mutabal carries tahini and yoghurt for a richer, paler dip; baba ghanoush is leaner, with parsley and tomato folded in. Lebanese and Syrian kitchens distinguish the two; some Western menus blur them.

The charred-skin technique is the dish. A grill, a gas hob, or a barbecue all work; an oven roast misses the smoke.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily for a mezze spread. The dip improves overnight in the fridge as the flavours settle.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better on day two. Make the morning of, or the day before; chill, then drizzle with fresh olive oil before serving.

recipeTools.notes: A gas hob or a barbecue gives the cleanest char. A grill works at a push.
For turning the aubergine over the flame without losing the skin.
Drains the bitter water out of the flesh.

glossaryTerms.definition: A paste of ground hulled sesame seeds, used across the Levant and the eastern Mediterranean. Pour off the oil layer that rises in the jar and stir hard to recombine before measuring; tahini that is dry at the bottom and oily at the top measures inaccurately.

content.text: Mutabal is a smoky aubergine dip from the Levant, whipped pale with tahini, lemon, garlic, and a spoon of yoghurt. The pith of it is the char on the skin: blackened over an open flame until the flesh inside softens and takes on the smoke. Everything after that is fifteen minutes of folding.
Mutabal is the richer Lebanese cousin of baba ghanoush. Both start with charred aubergine. Mutabal adds
tahini
and yoghurt for a paler, creamier dip; baba ghanoush stays leaner with parsley and tomato. Most Lebanese and Syrian cooks make both and keep them clearly separate.

The only technique that matters is the burning. A gas hob ring works; a barbecue is better. Oven-roasting an aubergine softens it but loses the smoke that defines the dish. If neither hob nor barbecue is to hand, the grill at full blast for fifteen minutes gets close.
What you need

items.prepNote: crushed to a paste with a pinch of salt
leaves picked and chopped, to finish

content.text: Method
Burn the aubergine

Set a gas hob ring to its highest flame. Pierce {{aubergine}} of aubergine in three or four places with a knife so the steam can escape. Lay one aubergine directly on the flame and let it burn. Turn every three or four minutes with tongs until the skin is uniformly black and the flesh has collapsed to the touch. Fifteen to twenty minutes; do the second aubergine the same way.

If you do not have a gas hob, place the aubergines on a foil-lined tray under the hottest grill, turning every five minutes until uniformly black. On a barbecue, lay them over the hottest coals and turn the same way.
Drain the flesh

Drop the burnt aubergines into a bowl and cover with a tea towel for fifteen minutes. The steam loosens the skin and the flesh continues to soften. When cool enough to handle, peel away the blackened skin and discard. Tip the flesh into a fine-mesh sieve set over a bowl. Press lightly with a fork to push out the bitter brown water. Let it drain for ten minutes.
Whip the dip

Tip the drained flesh into a medium bowl. Mash with a fork to a rough pulp. Add {{tahini}} of tahini, {{greek-yoghurt}} of yoghurt, the juice of {{lemon}} lemon, and the {{garlic}} of garlic paste. Beat with a wooden spoon for two minutes until pale and slightly fluffy. Taste; salt; taste again. Mutabal wants to be a touch oversalted to carry against bread; it dulls when chilled.
Chill, then garnish

Cover and chill for at least thirty minutes; overnight is better. To serve, spoon onto a wide flat plate and spread to a shallow disc with the back of a spoon. Drizzle {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil in a slow spiral. Sprinkle a pinch of paprika and the chopped parsley if using. Bring with warm pitta or flatbread.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: The dish turns on the char and the drain. Most failures trace to one of the two.

items.symptom: No smoke flavour, dip tastes flat.
items.cause: Aubergines roasted in the oven or undercharred on the flame.
items.fix: The skin needs to be uniformly black and slightly papery; the flesh needs to be completely collapsed. Under-charred aubergine produces a fresh, vegetable-flavoured dip rather than the smoky one.
Press the cooked flesh through a fine sieve for ten minutes. Tip away the brown water that collects. The dip should hold its shape on a spoon when finished.

items.cause: Skin not fully removed or burnt seeds left in.
items.fix: Pick over the flesh and remove any large clusters of dark seeds or fragments of charred skin. A small amount of seed is fine; a lot turns the dip bitter.

items.cause: Tahini was poorly stirred or too much was added.
items.fix: Stir tahini hard in the jar before measuring; the oil layer at the top has to recombine. If the finished dip stiffens, loosen with a spoon of cold water or lemon juice rather than more tahini.

content.text: Variations

For a vegan version, swap the yoghurt for an extra spoon of tahini and a teaspoon of cold water. Some Damascus cooks add a finely chopped tomato folded in at the end for colour and acid. A few pomegranate seeds and a drizzle of pomegranate syrup over the top works well.

Where mutabal differs from baba ghanoush: mutabal is paler, creamier, and tahini-led; baba ghanoush is leaner and herb-led with parsley and tomato folded through. Make both if you have the time, label them on the plate.
Where this dish lives

Mutabal is one of the standard cold mezze of Levantine kitchens: a dish that arrives at the start of a long meal, sat next to hummus, labneh, olives, and warm flatbread. The name comes from the Arabic root for spiced or seasoned. Sami Tamimi and Claudia Roden each give versions in their books; the dish predates both by centuries.

Lebanese kitchens carry the canonical recipe; Syrian and Palestinian variants are close to it; the Cypriot melitzanosalata is the same dish under a Greek name and with parsley folded in. Across the eastern Mediterranean, burning an aubergine and folding the smoke into a sauce is one of the older instincts, older than any written cookery.
